title-group.article-title: Determining the decreasing trend of maternal immunity against small ruminant morbillivirus in goat kids

aff.institution: Razi Vaccine and Serum Research, Education and Extension Organization (AREEO)
Department of Internal Medicine, University of Tehran

author-notes.corresp: *CorrespondenceMohsen Lotfi, Department of Quality Control, Razi Vaccine and Serum Research, Education and Extension Organization (AREEO), Karaj, Iran.Email: M.Lotfi@rvsri.ac.ir

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2023 The Authors. Veterinary Medicine and Science published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Small ruminant morbillivirus (SRMV) is the etiological agent of Peste des petits ruminants (PPR) disease. PPR is one of the most important viral diseases of small ruminant husbandry. In the endemic countries, vaccination is the main way to control this disease. Administering the first PPR vaccine in goat kids requires decreased maternal immunity.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: The aim of this study was to determine the decreasing trend of maternal immunity against SRMV in goat kids born from vaccinated goats.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Twenty Saanen goat kids were studied in two groups including control (n = 5, receiving colostrum from unvaccinated goats) and treatment (n = 15, receiving colostrum from vaccinated goats). Virus neutralisation (VN) test was used to evaluate serum specific antibodies against SRMV in goat kids from birth to 100 days of age.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The first goat kid (n = 1) in the treatment group was seronegative at the age of 28 days. All the goat kids were seronegative at the age of 100 days. The average serum titre of the goat kids at the age of 70–100 days became negative.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Some goat kids became seronegative before reaching the age of receiving the first PPR vaccine. The age of 70–100 days could be a good age range to give the first dose of PPR vaccine to the goat kids, but more studies were needed on the effectiveness of this vaccine at this age range.

p.mixed-citation: Abdollahi, M., Lotfi, M., Lotfollahzadeh, S., Adibi, M., Kamalzadeh, M., & Firuzyar, S. (2023). Determining the decreasing trend of maternal immunity against small ruminant morbillivirus in goat kids. Veterinary Medicine and Science, 9, 1818–1823. 10.1002/vms3.117937347592PMC10357234

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Peste des petits ruminants (PPR) (Kata) (Goat plague) is one of the most important viral diseases of small ruminant husbandry (Kabir et al., 2019). The etiological agent of this disease is small ruminant morbillivirus (SRMV), which belongs to the Morbillivirus genus and the Paramyxoviridae family (Waret‐Szkuta et al., 2008). Currently, PPR is endemic in many countries in Africa, the Middle East and South Asia, and there is concern about its spread in Europe (Constable et al., 2016; Mantip et al., 2022). Infection rates during outbreaks are high (above 50–90%) in endemic areas. Case fertility rate of the disease is 55–85% in goat and less than 10% in sheep (Constable et al., 2016).

The main way to prevent this disease in endemic countries is vaccination (Parida et al., 2015). In Iran, PPR vaccine is live attenuated and administered annually. The Nigerian 75.1 strain is used in the production of Iranian PPR vaccines. In most herds in Iran, attenuated live vaccines of goats and sheep, such as PPR vaccine and goat/sheep pox vaccines, are prescribed before the breeding season.

The immune system of neonates of small ruminants is not efficient enough at birth and matures over time (Dowling & Levy, 2014). During the neonatal period, maternal immunity is an extraordinary mechanism to protect neonates against some infectious pathogens (Hernandez‐Castellano et al., 2015). Like other ruminant neonates, goat kids receive maternal immunity through colostrum at birth (Matthews, 2016). Although maternal immunity is very important for the health of ruminant neonates, it must be reduced enough for vaccinations to be effective (Day & Schultz, 2014). This study was conducted with the aim of determining the decreasing trend of maternal immunity against PPR in goat kids.

sec.title: Study area
sec.p: This study was performed in a closed farm with 1500 Saanen goats during period from July 2021 to July 2022. The sample size was determined based on previous studies (Balamurugan et al., 2012; Kazeem et al., 2021; Markus et al., 2019).

sec.title: Animals
sec.p: Goat: The studied animals included 50 Saanen breed seronegative female goats aged 17 months. These animals were randomly (Elfil & Negida, 2017) divided into treatment (n = 35, receiving Nigerian 75/1 strain attenuated live PPR vaccine) and control (n = 12, receiving sodium chloride 0.9% as placebo) groups. Administration of vaccine and placebo was done 30 days before pregnancy, subcutaneously. Vaccine and placebo were injected subcutaneously in to the front of the shoulder. On the 40th day after artificial insemination (by laparoscopic method), singleton pregnancy of the studied animals was examined by ultrasonograhy (Buckrell, 1988). Single pregnant goats remained in the study, including 15 goats in the treatment group and 5 goats in the control group (Figure 1).

Goat kids: All kids born by the studied mothers had appropriate birth weight (2.5–3.5 kg) (Ince, 2010) and were included in the study. After birth, kids were fed enough with colostrum (200 mL immediately after birth and 210 mL/kg in the first 24 h) (Matthews, 2016). Kids born by the mothers receiving the vaccine were in the treatment group (n = 15 including 8 males and 7 females) and kids born by the mothers receiving the placebo (n = 5 including 3 males and 2 females) were in the control group. Serum IgG concentration at 48 h after birth showed that all the studied kids had no failure of passive transfer (FPT).

sec.title: Vaccination
sec.p: The national protocol of Iran for vaccination against PPR is to prescribe one dose of live attenuated vaccine per year. The administration of the mentioned vaccine is usually done before the breeding season (before pregnancy). In this study, it was tried to consider the conditions according to the industry. Thirty days before pregnancy, the studied mothers received a dose of vaccine or placebo. For vaccination, live attenuated Nigerian 75/1 strain PPR vaccine was used. Each dose of the vaccine contains at least 103 TCID50/mL of virus.

sec.title: Serum collection
sec.p: In does, blood samples were taken on the vaccination day and repeated 30 days after vaccination and 2 days before parturition (day 148 of pregnancy) (Figure 1). In newborn kids, the first blood sample was taken immediately after the birth )before receiving colostrum(, and then on days 3, 7, 14, 21, 28, 42, 56, 70 and 100 after the birth (Figure 2).

sec.title: Entry and exit criteria
sec.p: Inclusion criteria were determined for mothers (complete clinical health, singleton pregnancy, having a good BCS [3 on the 5‐point scale]) and for kids (complete clinical health, delivery without intervention, appropriate weight) (Abdollahi et al., 2022; Vries et al., 2014).
Exclusion criteria were determined for mothers (disease incidence) and for kids (FPT, disease incidence) (Abdollahi et al., 2022; Vries et al., 2014).

sec.title: Serological analysis
sec.p: Virus neutralisation (VN) test was used to determine the serum level of goat pox antibody (Balamurugan et al., 2012). Briefly, the sera were two‐fold serially diluted in 96‐well microplates. One hundred TCID50 of PPR vaccine virus Nigerian 75/1 strain were inoculated in each well. In the next step, the microplates were placed in an incubator (37°C, 60% humidity, 5% CO2, 1 h). Then, Vero cell suspension (105 cells in 100 μL) was added to each well and the plates were further incubated (37°C, 60% humidity, 5% CO2, 7 days). Then, the occurrence of cytopathic effects (CPE) in each well was investigated. Subsequently, the serum antibody titre was calculated by the Karber method and based on the following formula (Gholami et al., 2020).
VN50 = AntiLog(X – d/2 + [d × S]), X: the logarithm of the lowest dilution, d: the logarithm of the interval between dilutions, S: total negative cases divided by the number of wells per dilution.
According to the OIE instructions (OIE, 2017), VN50 > 10 (neutralising titre of greater than 10) was considered as positive.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: The normal distribution of data was confirmed using Shapiro–Wilk test. After plotting the serum antibody titre–time diagram in GraphPad Prism software (Version 9), independent t‐test in SPSS software (Version 22) was used for statistical analysis of data. Statistically significant differences were declared at a p value of less than 0.05.

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: In mothers, as shown in Table 1 and Figure 3, on day 180 before parturition, there was no significant difference in VN50 between the control and treatment groups (p > 0.05). On 150 and 2 days before parturition, VN50 was significantly higher in the treatment group as compared with VN50 in the control group (p < 0.05).

caption.p: Virus neutralisation index (VN50) in mothers and kids participating in the study (Mean ± SD).

fn.p: μ, mean; VN, virus neutralisation; +, positive; –, negative; C, control group; T, treatment group.

caption.p: Serum antibody titre–time diagram in mothers and kids participating in the study.

sec.p: In newborn kids, as shown in Table 1 and Figure 3, before receiving colostrum, VN50 was not significantly different between the control and treatment groups (p > 0.05). On days 3, 7, 14, 21, 28, 42, 56, 70 and 100 after birth, VN50 in kids in the treatment group was significantly higher than that of the kids in the control group (p < 0.05).

Serum antibody titre was positive in mothers after vaccine administration until delivery (Table 1, Figure 3). Average of serum antibody titre in kids was positive up to 70 days, and then, became negative (Table 1, Figure 3). The first kid with a negative serum antibody titre was observed at the age of 28 days and at the age of 100 days, and all the kids had negative serum antibody titres (Table 1, Figure 3).

The number of seronegative goat kids in the treatment group on days 28, 42, 56, 70 and 100 after birth was 1 (male), 2 (1 male and 1 female), 2 (1 male and 1 female), 2 (1 male and 1 female), and 15 (8 males and 7 females), respectively.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: PPR is one of the most important viral diseases in sheep and goats (Bazarghani et al., 2006). Currently, one of the most important issues in the small ruminant breeding industry in Iran is determining the appropriate age for the first vaccination of neonates against important infectious diseases (Abdollahi et al., 2022).

Studied vaccine caused the positive serum VN ability in all vaccinated female goats. This positive serum VN ability was stable for 6 months (until delivery). After receiving sufficient colostrum, all the studied goat kids reached the positive serum VN ability as their mothers. The first kids with negative serum VN ability were observed on the 28th day after birth. On the 100th day after birth, all the goat kids had negative serum VN ability. The average serum VN ability of the studied kids reached below the cut‐off at the age of 70–100 days after birth.

In the agreement of WOAH (The World Organisation for Animal Health) and FAO (The Food and Agriculture Organization) in the field of global eradication of PPR, the age of vaccination of goat kids is considered to be three months and above (Markus et al., 2019). This study showed that this targeting can be successful provided that it is accompanied by other biosecurity policies at the level of small ruminant farms because, in this study, the window of susceptibility to PPR in studied kids started at the age of 28 days.

In the study of Markus et al. (2019), the PPR vaccine (attenuated live Nigerian 75.1 strain) was administered to brown Kano goats in the third trimester of pregnancy. The susceptibility window of the goat kids started at 42 days old and reached 100% at the age of 84 days (Markus et al., 2019). The results of this study are consistent with the present study.

By the study of Balamurugan et al. (2012), it was shown that goat kids born from vaccinated goats had protective levels of maternal antibodies against SRMV for 4 months. The attenuated live vaccinal SRMV in the studied vaccine was Asian lineage IV virus. The results of this study are not consistent with the present study. Maybe the difference in the used vaccinal virus strain justifies this difference.

By the study of Kazeem et al. (2021), the susceptibility window to PPR in lambs born from ewes vaccinated with the attenuated live PPR vaccine of the Nigerien 75.1 strain was determined between 8 and 10 weeks and 9 weeks old, announced as a good time to administer the first PPR vaccine in lambs. The results of this study are consistent with the present study.

The results of the present study showed that the stability of antibody levels against PPR in mother goats was acceptable from the time of vaccine administration until the delivery time (6 months). This shows that administration of PPR vaccine around the time of delivery is not needed. This phenomenon is very helpful in health management of goat farms, because during this period (2 months before delivery), the farm veterinarian focuses on prescribing other vaccines (such as enterotoxaemia, contagious agalaxia etc.) while also thinking about other problems, such as hypocalcaemia, pregnancy toxaemia etc. Other studies on sheep and goat have also shown that antibody titres resulting from the administration of live attenuated PPR vaccines do not decrease significantly within 6 months after administration (Amanova et al., 2021; Kumar et al., 2014).

Based on the study of Bodjo et al. (2006), it was shown that if the attenuated live PPR vaccine (Nigerian 75/1 strain) was administered to pregnant ewes on the 90th and 120th days of pregnancy, their lambs became seronegative at the age of 75–90 days. The results of this study are consistent with the present study.

While the passive transfer of colostrum immunoglobulins is very important for the survival of the newborn, as long as these maternal immunoglobulins are not degraded enough, they inhibit the endogenous humoral immune response in the newborn. So, if there are high levels of maternal immunoglobulins in kids, a proper immune response to most vaccines will not be occurred (Balamurugan et al., 2012). The window of susceptibility is defined as the period of time when there is insufficient maternal immunoglobulin to protect against pathogens. The length of the susceptibility window period may vary greatly between individual animals and even between animals of the same breed. This phenomenon is related to some factors including the amount of antibodies in the mother's colostrum and the amount of colostrum eaten and absorbed by any young animals (Day & Schultz, 2014). In this study, the susceptibility window of goat kids to PPR started at 28 days after birth and was complete at 100 days after birth.

The present study showed that suitable time for first vaccination against PPR in the goat kids receiving sufficient amount of colostrum from vaccinated does is 70–100 days after the birth. But this finding applies to industrial farms where there is strong monitoring of receiving sufficient colostrum. According to the study of Abdollahi et al. (2020), in the conditions of traditional lamb and goat kid rearing system (semiintensive system) in Iran, about 50% of lambs and goat kids suffer from FPT because lambs and goat kids are with their mothers and there is no monitoring on receiving sufficient colostrum. Therefore, it is better to conduct another similar study in traditional (semi intensive) goat breeding systems.

In the present study, the number of male and female neonates in both control and case groups was almost equal. It seems that in the semiintensive system of sheep and goat breeding, the occurrence of FPT in male neonates is less (Abdollahi et al., 2020). In this study, colostrum was fed manually to the neonates in intensive system and there was no difference between the FPT rates in them. In addition, there was no obvious difference in the number of seronegative newborns in both studied groups.

Breed is one of the factors influencing the response to vaccination (Day & Schultz, 2014). One breed was used in this study. This highlights the importance of further studies on maternal immunity against PPR.

Adaptive immune responses are the basis of successful vaccination. Adaptive immunity has two types including type 1 immunity (called cell‐mediated immunity, mediated by type 1 helper cells, generates some antibodies, contains strong cytotoxic T cell responses and activates macrophages) and type 2 immunity (mediated by type 2 helper cells and generates much antibodies). Maternal antibodies inhibit neonatal antibody synthesis by acting through regulatory pathways ensuring that the body does not make more antibodies than it needs. They inhibit B cells, not T cells (Tizard, 2020). As a result, emergency vaccinations against PPR may have a level of effectiveness in younger goat kids born by vaccinated goats (goat kids with maternal immunity). This highlights the importance of further studies on effective vaccination against PPR in goat kids.

sec.title: CONCLUSION
sec.p: Based on the findings and discussion, it can be said that in the goat kids not suffering from FPT, it is recommended to administer the first PPR vaccine at the age of 70–100 days. In the goat kids not affected by FPT, the window of susceptibility to PPR starts at the age of 28 days and is completed at the age of 100 days.

sec.title: AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS
sec.p: Mostafa Abdollahi: conception and design (lead), collecting the data (lead), writing and revising the article (equal). Mohsen Lotfi: conception (equal), performing the experiment (lead), writing the article (lead). Samad Lotfollahzadeh: conception and design (equal), collecting the data (equal). Maryam Adibi: assistance in laboratory work (equal). Morteza Kamalzadeh: assistance in laboratory work (equal). Sajjad Firuzyar: assistance in laboratory work (equal).

sec.title: FUNDING
sec.p: There is no source of fund to declare.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest.

sec.title: ETHICS STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors confirm that the ethical policies of the journal, as noted on the journal's author guidelines page, have been adhered to and the appropriate ethical review committee approval has been received. The code of ethics in the research was taken from research committee of Ajdad Sepidan Kosar Company (ethics code: 1400/472).

sec.title: PEER REVIEW
sec.p: The peer review history for this article is available at https://publons.com/publon/10.1002/vms3.1179.

ack.title: ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS
ack.p: Ajdad Sepidan Kosar Company is appreciated for its cooperation in this research.

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
sec.p: The data that supports the findings of this study are available within the manuscript and also are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
